product.name: AIStoryTeller
product.tagline: A local story desk for scenes, world state, voices, and optional image generation.

hero.headline: A local-first story workbench that keeps text, world state, voice, and visual prompts in the same room.
hero.subhead: AIStoryTeller is a browser-based storytelling interface served from a local Python app. The repo shows an active prototype shaped around long sessions, reusable presets, optional memory, optional TTS, map and world tools, and optional image generation.

summary: This project reads like a serious local storytelling experiment rather than a polished platform pitch. It brings narrative generation, session state, starter content, optional memory retrieval, optional voices, and optional images into one browser-facing workflow.
audience: Writers who want a local story interface instead of a hosted writing tool
RPG tinkerers who care about scenes, parties, locations, and continuity
Builders studying how a local AI prototype grows from a single loop into a stacked system
coreProblems: Long story sessions lose coherence when the interface treats characters, locations, and status as disposable text.
Local creative tools often split writing, voice, memory, and visual prompting into separate apps.
Prototype-scale AI projects become hard to trust when the UI has no honest fallback for uncertain state.

features.title: Local story desk
features.summary: The documented entry point is a local Python server that opens a browser UI for story generation.

features.title: World and map layer
features.summary: The repo includes world management, map seeding, presets, and starter content instead of leaving state entirely inside prose.

features.title: Optional memory engine
features.summary: Infinite Memory Engine files, defaults, diagnostics, and helper model hooks show a continuity-focused path for longer sessions.

features.title: Voice track
features.summary: Local TTS support, voice instruction tools, samples, benchmarks, and cache logic are present in the repo.

features.title: Image sidecar
features.summary: Optional Stable Diffusion support, prompt guidance docs, and model registries let the story workflow branch into character or scene visuals.

workflows.title: Start from a preset or starter pack
workflows.summary: Story presets, global starters, and location or character seeds give the app a structured starting surface.

workflows.title: Run a scene in the browser
workflows.summary: The documented flow is a browser UI backed by local generation rather than a remote hosted dashboard.

workflows.title: Carry forward state
workflows.summary: World state, session history, map data, and optional memory tools are all visible in the repo history as first-class concerns.

workflows.title: Branch into voice or visuals
workflows.summary: When needed, the same project surface can add narrated output or prompt-driven image generation instead of forcing a separate tool chain.

proofPoints.label: Visible repo milestones
proofPoints.note: Counted from the current git history on 2026-03-17.

proofPoints.label: Tests in repo
proofPoints.note: Observed under /tests on 2026-03-17. Full test execution was unavailable because pytest was not installed in the active venv.

proofPoints.label: Smoke and diagnostic scripts
proofPoints.note: Image, IME, map, world, benchmark, and TTS helper scripts are present in /scripts.

proofPoints.label: Latest meaningful update
proofPoints.note: The last dated commit in visible history focused on runtime optimization.

faq.question: What kind of product is this?
faq.answer: Based on the repo, AIStoryTeller is best described as a local-first experimental web app. It serves a browser UI from a local Python runtime and combines story generation with optional world, voice, memory, and image tooling.

faq.question: Is this a finished release?
faq.answer: No clean release packaging is documented here. The safest public description is an active prototype with working surfaces and ongoing iteration.

faq.question: What platforms are clearly supported?
faq.answer: The browser UI is documented, and macOS is explicitly mentioned in the setup notes. Wider platform support may be possible, but it is not clearly documented enough to claim here.

faq.question: Does the project depend on hosted AI services?
faq.answer: The repo is built around local model placement and local serving, with OpenAI described as optional rather than primary.

faq.question: Are the visuals on this page literal screenshots?
faq.answer: No. This export uses calm product diagrams because the repo does not expose a clean public screenshot set suitable for publication.

screenshots.alt: Illustrated cover image showing a story page, a small map, and a voice strip gathered into one dark atlas-like frame.
screenshots.caption: Export cover art built from repo-backed themes, not a literal screenshot.

screenshots.alt: Illustrated session sheet showing story, map, starter pack, memory notes, and voice cues as connected panels.
screenshots.caption: Concept diagram showing how the repo's documented surfaces fit together.

catalog.title: AIStoryTeller
catalog.blurb: A local browser-based story workbench that keeps scenes, world state, voice, and visual prompting in one prototype surface.
catalog.platforms: Browser UI for a local Python runtime; macOS is explicitly documented.

repoArchetypeReasoning: README.md describes a local LLM story UI with optional Stable Diffusion image generation.
The documented run path is a local Python server that opens a browser address, which makes the safest primary category a web app.
The overall tone and git history still read like an active prototype, so the chosen framing is experimental rather than launch-style.
assumptions: Status is described as Active prototype because visible history continues through 2026-02-28 but no formal release markers are present.
Browser is listed as a platform because the documented local run path serves a browser UI.
No public-safe screenshot pack was available, so original SVG diagrams were created for this export.
missingButUsefulAssets: A public-safe browser screenshot of the actual story interface
